text: Revisional surgery in colorectal patients carries increased risk, including wound infection and breakdown. Multiple modalities have been employed to decrease surgical site infections. Adjuvant therapies include the use of vacuum-assisted wound devices, but necessitate leaving open the wound, resulting in time-consuming dressing changes, increased cost, length of stay, and patient discomfort.
We present the application of the Provenia incision management system for use in these patients due to its ease of application, low cost, and patient tolerance.

We present here a fifty-eight year old male with a history of transverse colon cancer. He underwent an open transverse colectomy with colocolic anastomosis at another institution. He subsequently developed further malignancies of his colon and thus underwent a completion total abdominal colectomy with iliorectal anastomosis and diverting loop ileostomy.
The surgeon measures the midline abdominal incision, which is visibly twenty-five centimeters in length, suitable for coverage with the standard pre-made dressing system.

It is important to communicate with stoma nursing preoperatively if a stoma will be needed during the surgery. As the pravena is a closed system, the hypercolloid ring around it must form an airtight seal with the skin. This requires approximately four centimeters or three finger breaths on each side. In general, a stoma should always be this far away when possible. Otherwise, regardless of dressing, this can result in wound complications.

The surgeon marks the stoma site, noting its medial position relative to the wound, and adjusts the stoma position laterally along the line within the rectus muscle. An incision is made away from the midline, and a closed suction drain is inserted into the pelvis, positioned laterally to avoid interference with the dressing.

The surgeon inserts a lap sponge into the abdomen and holds it against the abdominal wall at the intended stoma site. Using a #15 blade, a two-centimeter circular section of skin is excised, ensuring it is positioned far enough from the pravena system to prevent enteric content spillage.

The surgeon uses electrocautery to vertically dissect the subcutaneous tissues down to the fascia, which is incised superiorly and inferiorly. A Kelly clamp is inserted through the muscle to the posterior fascia, spreading the fibers bluntly. The posterior fascia is incised, and the Kelly clamp grasps the lap sponge to protect the bowel. The fascial opening is created to be approximately two centimeters in diameter to prevent strangulation or herniation of the loop ileostomy.

A lap sponge is placed through the abdominal defect and clamped to achieve hemostasis. The surgeon selects the appropriate section of bowel for the loop ileostomy, marking the distal end with a brown chromic tag and the proximal end with a blue vicryl to ensure correct orientation as it is pulled through the abdominal wall.

The surgeon uses a tonsil clamp to penetrate the mesentery directly beneath the mesointestinal border. A moist umbilical tape is threaded through the defect and used to gently pull the loop of bowel through the fascial opening.

The surgeon places a stoma rod after closing the midline incision with interrupted figure-of-eight sutures using O-sized monofilament absorbable sections, such as Maxon or PDS. The sutures are snapped and organized on a long clamp for proper order.

The surgeon closes the skin around the incision using surgical staples. The area is then cleaned and dried. A sponge is placed over the ostomy to provide a dry surface for the Pravena system to adhere to during dressing placement.

The surgeon matures the stoma after placing the Pravena system, applying Benzoin around the wound border to enhance adherence. The Pravena system is positioned over the incision, and its backing is removed before the dressing is placed over the wound.

The surgeon presses down firmly on the hypercolloid ring to ensure a seal, using the blunt end of an instrument to run around the sponge. The backing on the side sections of the dressing is removed, and a slit is cut out for the drain if necessary.

The surgeon places a stoma rod through the mesenteric defect, positioning it anteriorly to the dressing. Suction is applied to the dressing, and the stoma is matured. An appliance is placed over the Pravena dressing, which will remain in place until post-operative day seven. The incision is then left open to air.

If the patient is ready to be discharged prior to post-operative day seven, they may be discharged with a small vacuum canister to use at home. A randomized controlled trial is currently in progress at our institution to definitively confirm our initial promising results using the Pravena system for surgical site infection reduction. We believe that this system will prove to be a useful adjunct to standard treatment in high-risk patients or operations in our practice.
